passages.title: The Future Never Dies?
passages.source_html:
The Future Never Dies? 


The prospects for humanity and for the world as a whole are somewhere between glorious and dire. It is hard to be much more precise.


A 


By ‘glorious’, I mean that our descendants – all who are born on to this Earth – could live very comfortably and securely, and could continue to do so for as long as the Earth can support life, which should be for a very long time indeed. We should at least be thinking in terms of the next million years. Furthermore, our descendants could continue to enjoy the company of other species – establishing a much better relationship with them than we have now. Other animals need not live in constant fear of us. Many of those fellow species now seem bound to become extinct, but a significant proportion could and should continue to live alongside us. Such a future may seem ideal, and so it is. Yet I do not believe it is fanciful. There is nothing in the physical fabric of the Earth or in our own biology to suggest that this is not possible.


B 


‘Dire’ means that we human beings could be in deep trouble within the next few centuries, living but also dying in large numbers in political terror and from starvation, while huge numbers of our fellow creatures would simply disappear, leaving only the ones that we find convenient – chickens, cattle – or that we can’t shake off, like flies and mice. I’m taking it to be self-evident that glory is preferable.


C 


Our future is not entirely in our own hands because the Earth has its own rules, is part of the solar system and is neither stable nor innately safe. Other planets in the solar system are quite beyond habitation, because their temperature is far too high or too low to be endured, and ours, too, in principle could tip either way. Even relatively unspectacular changes in the atmosphere could do the trick. The core of the Earth is hot, which in many ways is good for living creatures, but every now and again, the molten rock bursts through volcanoes on the surface. Among the biggest volcanic eruptions in recent memory was Mount St Helens, in the USA, which threw out a cubic kilometre of ash – fortunately, in an area where very few people live. In 1815, Tambora (in present-day Indonesia) expelled so much ash into the upper atmosphere that climatic effects seriously harmed food production around the world for the season after season. Entire civilisations have been destroyed by volcanoes.


D 


Yet nothing we have so far experienced shows what volcanoes can really do. Yellowstone National Park in the USA occupies the caldera (the crater formed when a volcano collapses) of an exceedingly ancient volcano of extraordinary magnitude. Modem surveys show that its centre is now rising. Sometime in the next 200 million years, Yellowstone could erupt again, and when it does, the whole world will be transformed. Yellowstone could erupt tomorrow. But there’s a very good chance that it will give us another million years, and that surely is enough to be going on with. It seems sensible to assume that this will be the case.


E 


The universe at large is dangerous, too: in particular, we share the sky with vast numbers of asteroids, and now and again, the come into our planet’s atmosphere. An asteroid the size of a small island, hitting the Earth at 15,000 kilometres an hour (a relatively modest speed by the standards of heavenly bodies), would strike the ocean bed like a rock in a puddle, send a tidal wave around the world as high as a small mountain and as fast as a jumbo jet, and propel us into an ice age that could last for centuries. There are plans to head off such disasters (including rockets to push approaching asteroids into new trajectories), but in truth, it’s down to luck.


F 


On the other hand, the archaeological and the fossil evidence shows that no truly devastating asteroid has struck since the one that seems to have accounted for the extinction of the dinosaurs 65 million years ago. So again, there seems no immediate reason for despair. The Earth is indeed an uncertain place, in an uncertain universe, but with average luck, it should do us well enough. If the world does become inhospitable in the next few thousand or million years, then it will probably be our own fault. In short, despite the underlying uncertainty, our own future and that of our fellow creatures are very much in our own hands.


G 


Given average luck on the geological and the cosmic scale, the difference between glory and disaster will be made and is being made, by politics. Certain kinds of political systems and strategies would predispose us to long-term survival (and indeed to comfort and security and pleasure of being alive), while others would take us more and more frenetically towards collapse. The broad point is, though, that we need to look at ourselves – humanity – and at the world in general in a quite new light. Our material problems are fundamentally those of biology. We need to think, and we need our politicians to think, biologically. Do that, and take the ideas seriously, and we are in with a chance. Ignore biology and we and our fellow creatures haven’t a hope.

groups.title: Questions 14-26
groups.source_html:
Questions 14-19 


Do the following statements reflect the claims of the writer in the passage? In boxes 14-19 on your answer sheet write YES if the statement is ture NO if the statement is false NOT GIVEN if the information is not given in the passage

 


14

It seems predictable that some species will disappear.

正确答案： A. YES

原文答案句：Many of those fellow species now seem bound to become extinct, but a significant proportion could and should continue to live alongside us.

对应段落：A段

审题：题目问“似乎可以预测某些物种将会消失”，这一说法是否正确。

定位关键信息：利用题干中的"predictable"和"species will disappear"定位，在A段找到"seem bound to become extinct"（似乎注定会灭绝）。

解题思路：原文明确提到"seem bound to become extinct"（似乎注定会灭绝），与题干"predictable that some species will disappear"（可以预测某些物种会消失）意思一致，表明作者认为某些物种的消失是可以预测的。因此，题干表述与作者观点一致，选择YES。


15

The nature of the Earth and human biology make it impossible for human beings to survive another million years.

正确答案： B. NO

原文答案句：There is nothing in the physical fabric of the Earth or in our own biology to suggest that this is not possible.

对应段落：A段

审题：题目问“地球的本质和人类的生理特性注定人类无法再存续百万年之久”，这一说法是否正确。

定位关键信息：利用题干中的关键词“Earth”、“human biology”、“survive another million years”定位到A段。

解题思路：原文A段明确指出，毕竟地球的寿命应该非常漫长，我们至少应该以未来百万年为时间尺度来规划。地球的物理结构和我们自身的生物特性，都表明这是完全可能的。题干说“地球的本质和人类的生理特性注定人类无法再存续百万年之久”，而作者认为这是可能的，因此题干与作者观点相反，应选NO。


16

An eruption by Yellowstone is likely to be more destructive than previous volcanic eruptions.

正确答案： A. YES

原文答案句：Yellowstone National Park in the USA occupies the caldera (the crater formed when a volcano collapses) of an exceedingly ancient volcano of extraordinary magnitude. Modem surveys show that its centre is now rising. Sometime in the next 200 million years, Yellowstone could erupt again, and when it does, the whole world will be transformed.

对应段落：D段

审题：题目问黄石公园的火山爆发可能比之前的火山爆发更具破坏性，这一说法是否正确。

定位关键信息：通过题目的"Yellowstone"和"eruption"定位到D段，特别是"when it does, the whole world will be transformed"这句话表明黄石公园的火山爆发可能具有全球性的影响。

解题思路：原文中明确提到，现代地质调查显示，其核心区域正在持续隆起。未来2亿年间，黄石火山随时可能再度喷发，届时整个地球都将被彻底改变。而之前的火山爆发如圣海伦斯火山虽然也造成了破坏，但规模和影响远不及黄石公园的火山爆发。因此，题目的说法与原文一致，所以选择YES。


17

There is a greater chance of the Earth being hit by small asteroids than large ones.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

原文答案句：An asteroid the size of a small island, hitting the Earth at 15,000 kilometres an hour (a relatively modest speed by the standards of heavenly bodies), would strike the ocean bed like a rock in a puddle, send a tidal wave around the world as high as a small mountain and as fast as a jumbo jet, and propel us into an ice age that could last for centuries. 

对应段落：E段  

审题：题目问的是“小型小行星撞击地球的几率要大于大型小行星撞击地球的几率”，这一说法是否正确。

定位关键信息：通过题目的关键词“small asteroids”和“large ones”定位到E段，其中提到“vast numbers of asteroids”和“An asteroid the size of a small island”，但并未明确比较小型和大型小行星撞击地球的概率。  

解题思路：原文提到小行星的数量很多，并描述了一颗小岛大小的陨石撞击地球的后果，但并未提及不同大小的小行星撞击地球的概率。因此，相关信息未给出。答案为NOT GIVEN。


18

If the world becomes uninhabitable, it is most likely to be as a result of a natural disaster.

正确答案： B. NO

原文答案句：The Earth is indeed an uncertain place, in an uncertain universe, but with average luck, it should do us well enough. If the world does become inhospitable in the next few thousand or million years, then it will probably be our own fault.  

对应段落：F段  

审题：题目问的是“如果世界变得不宜居住，最有可能的原因是自然灾害”，需要判断这是否与作者的观点一致。  

定位关键信息：通过题干中的“uninhabitable”（不宜居住）和“natural disaster”（自然灾害）定位到F段。提到“如果世界在未来几千年或几百万年内变得不宜居住，那么这很可能是我们自己的错”。  

解题思路：原文明确提到“如果世界在未来几千年或几百万年内变得不宜居住，那么这很可能是我们自己的错”。  而非自然灾害。题目与原文观点相反，因此答案为NO。


19

Politicians currently in power seem unlikely to change their way of thinking.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

原文答案句：The broad point is, though, that we need to look at ourselves– humanity – and at the world in general in a quite new light. Our material problems are fundamentally those of biology. We need to think, and we need our politicians to think, biologically. Do that, and take the ideas seriously, and we are in with a chance. Ignore biology and we and our fellow creatures haven’t a hope.

对应段落：G段

审题：题目问的是“当前掌权的政治家似乎不太可能改变他们的思维方式”，需要判断这是否符合作者在文章中的观点。

定位关键信息：题目中的关键词是“politicians currently in power”和“seem unlikely to change their way of thinking”，通过这些关键词可以定位到G段中关于政治家思维方式的内容。

解题思路：在G段中，作者提到我们需要让政治家从生物学角度思考问题，并且如果他们能够这样做，我们就有机会。然而，作者并没有明确表示当前掌权的政治家是否不太可能改变他们的思维方式，只是提出了一个建议。因此，题目中的说法在文章中没有明确的支持或反驳，所以答案是NOT GIVEN。


Questions 20-25 


Complete the summary below. Choose NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS from the passage for each answer. Write your answers in boxes 20-25 on your answer sheet.

 


20

The Earth could become uninhabitable, like other planets, through a major change in the 20. .

正确答案： 20 temperature
Question 20

原文答案句：Other planets in the solar system are quite beyond habitation, because their temperature is far too high or too low to be endured, and ours, too, in principle could tip either way.  

对应段落：C段  

审题：题目问地球可能变得不适合居住，就像其他行星一样，是因为什么发生重大变化的。  

定位关键信息：题目中的"uninhabitable"和"planets"与C段第一句"Other planets in the solar system are quite beyond habitation"对应，定位到该句解释原因的部分。  

解题思路：原文明确提到其他行星不适合居住是因为它们的"temperature is far too high or too low"，即温度过高或过低，而地球也可能"tip either way"（向两个极端倾斜），说明地球变得不适合居住同样是因为温度的剧烈变化，因此答案为"temperature"。


21

Volcanic eruptions of 21.

正确答案： 21 molten rock#rock##ash
Question 21

原文答案句：The core of the Earth is hot, which in many ways is good for living creatures, but every now and again, the molten rock bursts through volcanoes on the surface.  

对应段落：C段  

审题：题目要求填空，问的是火山喷发会将什么物质喷发出来。  

定位关键信息：根据题目中的"volcanic eruptions"定位到C段，该段描述了火山喷发的原因和影响。  

解题思路：原文中提到，地球的核心是热的，这在很多方面对生物是有益的，但每隔一段时间，熔岩就会从地表的火山中喷发出来。火山喷发由"molten rock"（熔岩）从地核中喷出引起的，因此答案为"molten rock"。


22

can lead to shortages of 22. in a wide area.

正确答案： 22 food
Question 22

原文答案句：In 1815, Tambora (in present-day Indonesia) expelled so much ash into the upper atmosphere that climatic effects seriously harmed food production around the world for the season after season.  

对应段落：C段  

审题：题目问的是火山喷发可能导致大面积的什么短缺。  

定位关键信息：根据题干中的"shortages"（短缺）和"wide area"（大面积）定位到C段，该段提到火山喷发对食物生产的影响。  

解题思路：原文中提到坦博拉火山喷发将大量火山灰喷入大气层，导致全球气候影响，严重破坏了食物生产，这与题干描述的情况一致。因此，答案为"food"。


23

An asteroid hitting the Earth could create a 23.

正确答案： 23 tidal wave
Question 23

原文答案句：An asteroid the size of a small island, hitting the Earth at 15,000 kilometres an hour (a relatively modest speed by the standards of heavenly bodies), would strike the ocean bed like a rock in a puddle, send a tidal wave around the world as high as a small mountain and as fast as a jumbo jet, and propel us into an ice age that could last for centuries.  

对应段落：E段  

审题：题目要求填空，描述小行星撞击地球可能产生的现象。  

定位关键信息：通过题干中的"An asteroid hitting the Earth"定位到E段，找到描述小行星撞击地球后果的句子。  

解题思路：原文中明确提到，一颗小岛大小的陨石，若以每小时15,000公里（按天体速度标准来看不算快）的速度撞击地球，其威力堪比水坑中的巨石，不仅会引发全球范围内的海啸"send a tidal wave around the world"，因此答案为tidal wave。


24

that would result in a new 24. .

正确答案： 24 Ice age
Question 24

原文答案句：An asteroid the size of a small island, hitting the Earth at 15,000 kilometres an hour (a relatively modest speed by the standards of heavenly bodies), would strike the ocean bed like a rock in a puddle, send a tidal wave around the world as high as a small mountain and as fast as a jumbo jet, and propel us into an ice age that could last for centuries.  

对应段落：E段  

审题：题目要求根据填空题的题干，小行星撞击会导致新的什么。  

定位关键信息：题干中的“result in a new”提示我们需要寻找一个由动词或名词引导的结果状语从句或名词短语，且该短语描述的是一种自然现象。结合文章内容，E段提到了小行星撞击地球的后果，其中“propel us into an ice age”直接对应“导致一种新的现象”。  

解题思路：原文E段描述了小行星撞击地球的连锁反应，其中明确提到将把人类推向可能延续数百年的冰河时代，“propel us into an ice age”。题干中的“result in a new”与原文的“propel us into”在语义上一致。因此，答案为“ice age”。


25

Plans are being made to use 25. to deflect asteroids heading for the Earth. on buildings and the joyful smell from the sea.

正确答案： 25 rockets
Question 25

原文答案句：There are plans to head off such disasters (including rockets to push approaching asteroids into new trajectories), but in truth, it’s down to luck.  

对应段落：E段  

审题：题目问的是“计划使用什么来偏转朝地球飞来的小行星”。  

定位关键信息：根据题干中的“Plans are being made to use”和“deflect asteroids heading for the Earth”可以定位到E段中关于小行星威胁和应对计划的描述。  

解题思路：原文中明确提到“plans to head off such disasters (including rockets to push approaching asteroids into new trajectories)”，有计划通过发射火箭将接近的小行星推入新轨道来避免此类灾难。因此，答案为“rockets”。


Question 26 


Choose the correct letter, A, B, C or D. Write the correct letter in the box 26 on your answer sheet.

 


26

What is the writer’s purpose in the passage?

正确答案： D. to argue the need for a general change in behavior

原文答案句：The broad point is, though, that we need to look at ourselves – humanity – and at the world in general in a quite new light. Our material problems are fundamentally those of biology. We need to think, and we need our politicians to think, biologically. Do that, and take the ideas seriously, and we are in with a chance. Ignore biology and we and our fellow creatures haven’t a hope.  

对应段落：G段  

审题：题目问的是作者写这篇文章的目的。  

定位关键信息：通过阅读全文，尤其是最后一段，可以定位到作者表达的核心观点。  

解题思路：文章开头提出人类和世界的未来可能既光明，也危险，接着分别阐述了这两种可能性的原因，包括地球自身的规则、火山活动、宇宙中的小行星等自然因素，以及政治和人类行为等人为因素。最后一段明确指出，尽管存在不确定性，但人类的未来很大程度上掌握在自己手中，需要从生物学角度思考问题，改变行为方式。因此，作者写这篇文章的目的是论证需要从根本上改变人类的行为方式，以应对未来的挑战。这与选项D的表述一致。
